title: Diagram groups and directed 2-complexes: homotopy and homology
overall_summary: The paper reformulates diagram groups in terms of directed 2-complexes, where 2-paths are directed homotopies between directed 1-paths and diagrams are planar representatives modulo dipole cancellation. It constructs a multidimensional Squier semi-cubical complex Sq(K), identifies it with a space of positive paths in K, and proves that its fundamental groups are exactly the diagram groups. The most relevant contractibility result is that the universal cover of every connected component Sq(K,p) is contractible; equivalently, Sq(K,p) is a K(D(K,p),1). The proof uses universal 2-covers, which are rooted 2-trees, and a direct deformation retraction of their positive path spaces along the natural rooted-tree filtration. The paper also develops a collapsing-scheme/discrete-Morse method for complete directed 2-complexes, producing smaller K(G,1) complexes with cells indexed by essential cubes; this may be useful if the matching complex in the original problem can be encoded as a Squier-type cubical complex or as a universal cover/component thereof.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex. An atomic 2-path is a triple (p,f,q), where p and q are 1-paths in K and f is a 2-cell, with top 1-path p⌈f⌉q and bottom 1-path p⌊f⌋q. The isotopy relation on 2-paths is the smallest equivalence relation that is invariant under concatenation and contains all pairs obtained by interchanging two independent atomic 2-paths: for 2-cells f1 and f2 and 1-paths p,q,r for which all concatenations are defined, the 2-path (p,f1,q⌈f2⌉r) ◦ (p⌊f1⌋q,f2,r) is isotopic to (p⌈f1⌉q,f2,r) ◦ (p,f1,q⌊f2⌋r).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Each atomic 2-path is represented by a one-cell planar diagram. Concatenating 2-paths corresponds to gluing the bottom path of one diagram to the top path of the next. If two atomic replacements occur in disjoint subpaths, the same two-cell planar diagram can be read in two vertical orders, giving exactly the displayed pair of 2-paths. Conversely, any isotopy of a diagram preserves the planar cell structure but may change the order in which cells are read from top to bottom. The possible changes of reading order are generated by adjacent swaps of cells that are incomparable in the planar dependence order, and such cells are precisely independent atomic 2-paths. Closure under concatenation accounts for performing these swaps inside longer 2-paths.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.5 and Theorem 2.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex. For 1-paths u and v in K, u is homotopic to v by a 2-path if and only if there exists a planar (u,v)-diagram over K. A dipole in a diagram is a pair of cells π1,π2 such that the bottom path of π1 coincides with the top path of π2 and the labels of π1 and π2 are mutually inverse; reducing a dipole removes the two cells and identifies the remaining boundary paths. Every equivalence class of diagrams over K contains exactly one reduced diagram, meaning a diagram with no dipoles. Equivalently, every 2-path is homotopic to a reduced 2-path, and two homotopic reduced 2-paths have equal diagrams and the same number of atomic factors. Thus morphisms in the diagram groupoid D(K) can be represented by reduced diagrams, with multiplication given by concatenation followed by dipole reduction.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given a 2-path, glue the atomic diagrams of its atomic factors along matching top and bottom 1-paths; this produces a planar diagram with top u and bottom v. Conversely, a planar diagram satisfying the directed conditions can be peeled or read cell by cell, yielding a sequence of atomic replacements, hence a 2-path. Dipole cancellation corresponds exactly to deleting a subword δδ^{-1} in a 2-path. The number of cells strictly decreases under dipole cancellation, so the process terminates. The uniqueness of the reduced form is the Kilibarda normal-form lemma: different possible dipole reductions are compatible, because disjoint cancellations commute and the planar structure prevents incompatible overlap from producing distinct reduced outcomes. Therefore two diagrams are equivalent precisely when their reduced forms coincide.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex with a chosen orientation of its 2-cells, and let p be a 1-path in K. Define the Squier semi-cubical complex Sq(K) as follows: its 0-cubes are 1-paths in K, and its n-cubes for n ≥ 1 are thin diagrams ε(u0)+f1+ε(u1)+...+fn+ε(un), where u0,...,un are 1-paths and f1,...,fn are negative 2-cells of K. The face maps replace the i-th cell fi by its top or bottom path. Let Sq(K,p) be the connected component containing p. Then the diagram group D(K,p), the local group of reduced spherical (p,p)-diagrams over K, is isomorphic to the fundamental group π1(Sq(K),p), equivalently π1(Sq(K,p),p).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The 1-skeleton of Sq(K) has directed edges corresponding to atomic 2-paths (u,f,v), and an edge path in Sq(K,p) therefore determines a 2-path in K starting at p. A loop based at p determines a spherical (p,p)-diagram, giving a homomorphism π1(Sq(K,p),p) to D(K,p). The 2-cells of Sq(K) correspond to pairs of independent atomic 2-paths, and their boundaries encode exactly the commutation relation from Lemma 2.2, so the map respects homotopy of loops. Surjectivity follows because every spherical diagram can be read as a sequence of atomic 2-paths, hence as an edge loop. Injectivity follows because if the resulting diagram is trivial in D(K,p), then the sequence of atomic moves can be transformed to the trivial sequence by swaps of independent moves and cancellations δδ^{-1}; the swaps are filled by Squier 2-cells and the cancellations give backtracking in the 1-skeleton. Higher-dimensional cubes do not change π1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex, and let p and q be homotopic 1-paths in K. Then the diagram groups D(K,p) and D(K,q) are isomorphic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If p and q are homotopic, they lie in the same connected component of Sq(K). By Theorem 3.4, D(K,p) and D(K,q) are the fundamental groups of this connected component with different basepoints, and fundamental groups of a path-connected space with different basepoints are isomorphic after choosing a path between the basepoints.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex. A positive path in K is a finite concatenation of ordinary 1-paths and paths ft through 2-cells f, where t ∈ [0,1] parametrizes the horizontal slice of the cell and f0 = ⌈f⌉, f1 = ⌊f⌋ for a positive cell f, with the convention (f^{-1})t = f1−t. Let Ω+(K) be the set of all positive paths. Every positive path has a unique normal form u0 f1,t1 u1 ... fm,tm um with fi negative 2-cells and ti ∈ (0,1). The map ξ sending this normal form to the point with coordinates (t1,...,tm) in the cube ε(u0)+f1+ε(u1)+...+fm+ε(um) is a bijection from Ω+(K) to the geometric realization of Sq(K). More generally, if some ti are 0 or 1, the same coordinate rule agrees with the face identifications of Sq(K).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Each point in the realization of Sq(K) lies in the interior of a unique cube, and its cube coordinates determine a unique positive path in normal form, giving an inverse to ξ. If a positive path is written with some parameter ti equal to 0 or 1, replacing fi,0 by ⌈fi⌉ or fi,1 by ⌊fi⌋ passes to a face of the corresponding cube. The definition of the Squier face maps identifies precisely that face with the cube obtained after this replacement. Therefore the coordinate assignment is independent of the chosen non-normal expression and gives a well-defined bijection.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1 and Lemma 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex. First, if u is a nonempty 1-path in K and K′ is obtained from K by adding a new edge e with the same endpoints as u and a new 2-cell f of the form u = e, then for every 1-path w in K the diagram groups D(K,w) and D(K′,w) are isomorphic. Second, suppose K is the union of directed 2-complexes K1 and K2 that have the same vertices and edges as K, and suppose that for each positive 2-cell f of K1, the paths ⌈f⌉ and ⌊f⌋ are homotopic in K2 to paths uf and vf, respectively. If K′ is obtained by keeping K2 and replacing each such cell f by a cell uf = vf, then D(K,w) is isomorphic to D(K′,w) for every 1-path w. Consequently, if K′ is a subdivision of K obtained by cutting 2-cells by adding intermediate edges and cells, then D(K,w) ≅ D(K′,w) for every nonempty 1-path w in K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the edge-adjunction move, the inclusion K ⊂ K′ gives a natural homomorphism D(K,w) to D(K′,w). A reduced (w,w)-diagram over K′ whose boundary labels lie in K cannot contain the new edge e: any occurrence of e is internal and adjacent to two cells, and the only cells containing e are f and f^{-1}, so the two adjacent cells form a dipole. Thus every reduced spherical diagram over K′ with boundary w is already over K, proving surjectivity and injectivity. For the replacement move, choose diagrams Γf over K2 from ⌈f⌉ to uf and Δf over K2 from ⌊f⌋ to vf. Define a map from diagrams over K to diagrams over K′ by replacing each cell f by Γf followed by the new cell uf = vf followed by Δf^{-1}; define the inverse map by replacing the new cell by Γf^{-1}, then the old cell f, then Δf. Compositions produce diagrams differing from the identity by subdiagrams Γf Γf^{-1} or Δf Δf^{-1}, which reduce by dipole cancellation, so the induced diagram-group homomorphisms are mutually inverse. A subdivision is obtained by combining these moves: add an intermediate edge and one half-cell, then replace the old cell by the other half-cell.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let φ: K → K′ be a morphism of directed 2-complexes, meaning that vertices map to vertices, edges map to nonempty 1-paths, and 2-cells map to 2-paths compatibly with top, bottom, initial, terminal, and inverse operations. For a 1-path p in K, φ induces a homomorphism φp: D(K,p) → D(K′,φ(p)). The following hold. (1) If φ(δ) is reduced for every reduced 2-path δ in K and φ(f) is nonempty for every 2-cell f of K, then φ is nonsingular, i.e. φp is injective for every p. (2) If φ is injective on the set of 2-cells and φ(f) is a single 2-cell for every 2-cell f of K, then φ is nonsingular. (3) If K′ is obtained from K by adding 2-cells, then each D(K,p) naturally embeds in D(K′,p). (4) If K′ is obtained from K by adding 2-cells and for every new 2-cell f the paths ⌈f⌉ and ⌊f⌋ are homotopic in K, then D(K,p) is a retract of D(K′,p) for every 1-path p in K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1), if a nontrivial element of D(K,p) were in the kernel, choose its unique reduced diagram or reduced 2-path δ. By hypothesis φ(δ) is reduced and nonempty, so by uniqueness of reduced diagrams it cannot represent the identity in D(K′,φ(p)). For (2), replacing each cell of a reduced diagram by a distinct single cell cannot create a dipole, because a dipole in the image would come from a dipole in the original diagram with inverse labels; hence (1) applies. Part (3) is the special case of (2) for the inclusion map. For (4), choose for every new cell f a fixed diagram Γf over K connecting ⌈f⌉ to ⌊f⌋, with inverse diagrams assigned to inverse cells. Replacing every new cell in a diagram over K′ by Γf defines a homomorphism D(K′,p) → D(K,p) that restricts to the identity on D(K,p), hence a retraction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a complete directed 2-complex, meaning that it is Noetherian with respect to positive 2-path reductions and confluent. A left forest in Sq(K) is a spanning forest T such that whenever an edge (p,f,q) lies in T, the 1-path p is irreducible, and then every edge (p,f,q′) also lies in T. A right forest is defined symmetrically using the right context q. Then Sq(K) has both a left forest and a right forest.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In each connected component choose the unique irreducible vertex, which exists by Noetherianity and confluence. For every non-irreducible 1-path p, choose its shortest non-irreducible initial segment. This segment has the form u⌊f⌋ for some negative 2-cell f with u irreducible, so p = u⌊f⌋v; assign to p the edge (u,f,v). Let T contain all vertices and all assigned edges together with their inverses. Following assigned edges moves a vertex toward its irreducible normal form, so no cycle can occur and every vertex is connected to the chosen irreducible vertex in its component. The construction gives condition F1 by the choice of u, and condition F2 because the assignment depends only on the left irreducible prefix and the cell, not on the remaining right context. The right forest is obtained by the analogous construction using shortest non-irreducible terminal segments.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.1 and Theorem 7.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A rooted 2-tree with root 1-path w is a directed 2-complex K given as an ascending union K0 ⊂ K1 ⊂ K2 ⊂ ... where K0 is the simple directed path w, and Kn+1 is obtained from Kn by adding positive 2-cells f whose top paths ⌈f⌉ lie in Kn and whose bottom paths ⌊f⌋ are new simple arcs meeting Kn only at endpoints, with distinct new bottom arcs disjoint except possibly at endpoints. For a directed 2-complex K with distinguished 1-path w, consider: T1, every vertex of K lies on some 1-path from ι(w) to τ(w); T2, any two 1-paths in K with the same endpoints are homotopic in K; T3, the diagram group D(K,w) is trivial. Lemma 7.1 states that every rooted 2-tree with root w satisfies T1, T2, and T3. Theorem 7.2 states conversely that if K with distinguished path w satisfies T1, T2, and T3, then K is a rooted 2-tree with root w.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 7.1, argue by induction over the filtration. T1 is preserved because a new vertex lies on the new bottom arc of some cell f, and the endpoints of that arc already lie on root-to-root paths in the previous stage. For T2, induct on the number of new edges from Kn+1 − Kn appearing in two paths with the same endpoints. If one path contains a new edge, then because new arcs meet the old complex only at endpoints, the path contains the whole bottom arc of a newly attached cell; replacing that bottom arc by the top path reduces the number of new edges and preserves homotopy. For T3, a reduced nontrivial spherical diagram over Kn+1 with boundary w must contain a cell from Kn+1 − Kn. The bottom path of such a cell is a new simple arc, so it must be adjacent to an inverse cell, forming a dipole, contradiction. For Theorem 7.2, define K0 to be the path w and let Kn+1 be obtained by adjoining all 2-cells of K whose top paths lie in Kn. Conditions T1 and T2 ensure that the union of the Kn is all of K. The key claim is that if M is already a rooted 2-tree with root w and f is a 2-cell outside M with top in M, then no internal vertex of ⌊f⌋ lies in M. If such a vertex o existed, split ⌊f⌋ at o. Using T1 inside M and T2 in K, build diagrams connecting the split pieces to paths in M, then combine them with the cell f and diagrams in M to obtain a nontrivial spherical (w,w)-diagram containing f but no f^{-1}. After reducing dipoles, a nontrivial reduced spherical diagram remains, contradicting T3. Hence each new bottom path is a fresh simple arc, so the filtration is exactly a rooted 2-tree filtration.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex and p a 1-path in K. A directed 2-complex M over K has a labelling map φ: M → K sending edges to edges and 2-cells to 2-cells. For a 1-path r in M, the 2-star of r is the set of atomic 2-paths in M with top r, and φ induces a local map from the 2-star of r to the 2-star of φ(r). A universal 2-cover of K with base p is a directed 2-complex M over K satisfying U1, M is a rooted 2-tree with root path p̃ labelled by p, and U2, for every 1-path q̃ in M from ι(p̃) to τ(p̃), the local map at q̃ is bijective. For every K and p, a universal 2-cover exists and is unique up to label-preserving isomorphism.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence is by induction. Start with M0 equal to a simple path p̃ labelled by p. Given Mn, for every pair (r̃,f) where r̃ is a 1-path in Mn, f is a 2-cell of K, and ⌈f⌉ = φ(r̃), if Mn does not already contain a 2-cell labelled f with top r̃, attach a new 2-cell f̃ with top r̃ and bottom a fresh simple arc labelled ⌊f⌋. Do this for all such pairs, with new arcs disjoint except at endpoints, to obtain Mn+1. The union M is a rooted 2-tree. The construction gives property U2′: for every 1-path r̃ in M and every 2-cell f of K with ⌈f⌉ = φ(r̃), there is exactly one 2-cell of M labelled f with top r̃. Surjectivity of the local map at q̃ follows by decomposing any atomic 2-path in K with top φ(q̃) as (u,f,v) and applying U2′ to the corresponding subpath of q̃; injectivity follows because two preimages would give two cells with the same top and label. For uniqueness, first show that any universal 2-cover satisfying U1 and U2 also satisfies U2′ by extending r̃ to a root-to-root path and applying local bijectivity. Then compare the natural rooted-tree filtrations of two universal 2-covers by induction: the roots are label-preservingly isomorphic, and U2′ gives a unique corresponding new cell with the same top and label at each next stage.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let eKp be the universal 2-cover of a directed 2-complex K with base 1-path p, with root p̃ and labelling map φ: eKp → K. For every 2-path δ in K with top ⌈δ⌉ = p, there exists a unique 2-path δ̃ in eKp with top ⌈δ̃⌉ = p̃ and φ(δ̃) = δ. Consequently, for every (p,q)-diagram Δ over K there exists a unique 1-path q̃ in eKp and a unique (p̃,q̃)-diagram Δ̃ over eKp such that φ(Δ̃) = Δ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lift δ atom by atom. Suppose an initial segment has been lifted and ends at a lifted 1-path r̃. The next atomic 2-path in δ lies in the 2-star of φ(r̃), and U2 gives a unique preimage in the 2-star of r̃. This proves existence and uniqueness of the lifted 2-path. A diagram Δ corresponds to a 2-path δ, so lifting δ gives a diagram Δ̃ over eKp. If a different 2-path represents the same diagram, it differs by isotopies generated by swaps of independent atomic 2-paths; local maps preserve independence, so the lifted 2-paths are isotopic and have the same diagram. Thus the lift of the diagram is unique.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a directed 2-complex, p a 1-path in K, and eKp the universal 2-cover of K with root p̃. Then the connected component Sq(eKp,p̃) of the Squier complex of eKp is homeomorphic to the universal cover of the connected component Sq(K,p) of the Squier complex of K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the positive-path realization of Squier complexes. A positive path q̃ in the component of p̃ in Ω+(eKp) has a unique expression q̃ = u0 f1,t1 u1 ... fm,tm um, with fi 2-cells in eKp and ti ∈ (0,1). Let r̃ = u0⌈f1⌉u1...⌈fm⌉um and let Ψ̃ = ε(u0)+f1+ε(u1)+...+fm+ε(um) be the corresponding thin diagram. Since eKp is a rooted 2-tree, properties T2 and T3 give a unique reduced diagram Δ̃ from p̃ to r̃. Applying the labelling map φ gives a diagram Δ over K from p to r and a thin diagram Ψ over K such that Δ ◦ Ψ has no dipoles. Farley's model of the universal cover of Sq(K,p) has cubes represented by such pairs (Δ,Ψ); the point corresponding to q̃ is the point with coordinates (t1,...,tm) in that cube. The inverse map is obtained by uniquely lifting diagrams and thin diagrams from K to eKp using Lemma 8.3. Face identifications match because setting ti to 0 or 1 replaces fi by its top or bottom path on both sides.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.5 and Remark 8.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every directed 2-complex K and every 1-path p in K, the universal cover of Sq(K,p) is contractible. Hence Sq(K,p) is a K(G,1) CW complex for the diagram group G = D(K,p).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The theorem is stated as Farley's contractibility theorem, and the paper also gives a direct proof using Theorem 8.4. By Theorem 8.4, the universal cover of Sq(K,p) is Sq(eKp,p̃), equivalently the component of p̃ in Ω+(eKp). Let K0 ⊂ K1 ⊂ ... be the natural rooted-tree filtration of eKp and let Ωn be the subspace of positive paths in the component of p̃ that lie in Kn. Then Ω0 is the single point p̃. For h ∈ Ωn+1, write h uniquely as h = v0 g1,s1 v1 ... gk,sk vk, where the vi are positive paths in Kn, the gi are positive 2-cells in Kn+1 − Kn in the natural orientation, and si ∈ (0,1]. Define hs = v0 g1,ss1 v1 ... gk,ssk vk for s ∈ [0,1]. At s=1 this is h, at s=0 each new-cell segment is replaced by its top path in Kn, so h0 ∈ Ωn, and if h already lies in Ωn then hs = h for all s. This gives a deformation retraction Ωn+1 → Ωn for every n. Since the component is the ascending union of these Ωn and Ω0 is a point, it is contractible. The K(G,1) conclusion follows from Theorem 3.4, which identifies π1(Sq(K,p),p) with D(K,p).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 9.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a semi-cubical complex. Suppose the cubes of X are partitioned into essential, collapsible, and redundant cubes; suppose there is a strict partial order ≻ on redundant cubes satisfying the descending chain condition; and suppose there is a bijection c ↦ ĉ from redundant n-cubes to collapsible (n+1)-cubes for all n such that c occurs exactly once among the n-faces of ĉ and every other redundant n-face c′ of ĉ satisfies c ≻ c′. Then X is homotopy equivalent to a CW complex Y whose n-cells are in one-to-one correspondence with the essential n-cubes of X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a cubical collapsing-scheme or discrete-Morse argument. For each redundant cube c, its paired collapsible cube ĉ has c as a free face after all smaller redundant faces have been dealt with. The descending chain condition ensures that the required elementary collapses can be ordered so that no infinite descending obstruction occurs. Performing these collapses dimension by dimension removes every collapsible cube together with its redundant free face and preserves homotopy type at each elementary step. The remaining cells are exactly the essential cubes, and their attaching maps define the desired CW complex Y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a complete directed 2-complex, let w be a nonempty 1-path in K, and let G = D(K,w). Fix a left forest Tl in Sq(K). For an n-cube c = ε(u0)+f1+...+fn+ε(un) of Sq(K), where the fi are negative 2-cells, call a term ui special if ui is not irreducible, and call a term fi special if the edge (ui−1,fi,*) belongs to Tl. Then there exists a K(G,1) CW complex Yw whose n-cells are in one-to-one correspondence with the thin diagrams c = ε(u0)+f1+...+fn+ε(un) such that all ui are irreducible, no edge (ui−1,fi,*) lies in Tl, and the top path ⌈c⌉ is homotopic to w in K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Lemma 9.1 to Sq(K,w). Classify a cube as essential if it has no special term. If it has a special term, inspect the leftmost special term: if it is some ui, call the cube redundant; if it is some fi, call it collapsible. Define a partial order on redundant cubes by c ≻ c′ if ⌊c⌋ reduces positively to ⌊c′⌋, and if the bottom paths are equal, by moving cells to the right in the thin diagram. Noetherianity of K and a lexicographic length decrease for right-moving cells give the descending chain condition. For a redundant cube c whose leftmost special term is ui, let (p,f,q) be the left-forest edge assigned to ui, so ui = p⌊f⌋q. Insert the cell f at that position to get a collapsible cube ĉ. Its face obtained by replacing f by its bottom path is exactly c; all other redundant faces either have more reduced bottom path or are obtained by moving a cell to the right, hence are smaller. This gives the required collapsing scheme. Since Sq(K,w) is already a K(G,1) by Theorem 8.5, the homotopy equivalent complex Yw is also a K(G,1), with cells indexed by the essential cubes described.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a complete directed 2-complex, let w be a nonempty 1-path in K, and let G = D(K,w). With the same notion of essential cubes in Sq(K,w) determined by a left forest as in Theorem 9.2, the integer homology group Hn(G; Z) is free Abelian for every n ≥ 0, and a free basis for Hn(G; Z) is given by the essential n-cubes of Sq(K,w).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the cubical cellular chain complex Pn freely generated by n-cubes of Sq(K,w), with boundary ∂c = Σi (−1)^i(⌈c⌉i − ⌊c⌋i). The collapsing scheme from Theorem 9.2 defines a chain-equivalent complex Q generated by essential cubes via an endomorphism φ: P → P: φ is the identity on essential cubes, zero on collapsible cubes, and on a redundant cube c is defined recursively from the relation ∂ĉ = ±c + lower terms for its paired collapsible cube ĉ. The key lemma in the proof states that if c = ε(u0)+f1+...+fn+ε(un) and cbar is obtained by replacing every ui by its irreducible form, then φ(c) = cbar if cbar is essential and φ(c) = 0 if cbar is collapsible. This is proved by Noetherian induction, using the ordering from the collapsing scheme and the fact that opposite faces of a thin cube have the same irreducible replacement. It follows that φ(⌈c⌉i) = φ(⌊c⌋i) for every face pair, so the induced differential δ = φ∂ on Q is identically zero. Therefore Hn(G; Z) is Qn itself, the free Abelian group on essential n-cubes.

proof_techniques.name: Encode local rewrites as a semi-cubical Squier complex
proof_techniques.description: The paper builds Sq(K) from local directed homotopies in a directed 2-complex K. Vertices are 1-paths; edges are atomic replacements of a subpath ⌈f⌉ by ⌊f⌋; higher cubes are thin diagrams consisting of several mutually separated 2-cells, and each facet is obtained by resolving one of those cells to its top or bottom path. The attaching maps are canonical because replacing one independent cell does not affect the coordinates of the others. This construction is precisely designed to turn a system of local moves with commuting independent moves into a cubical or semi-cubical complex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The complex F_w also has k-cells indexed by objects with k independent local binary resolutions, namely crossings of a matching. A useful route is to identify matchings and crossing resolutions with thin diagrams or simultaneous local rewrites, so that F_w becomes a Squier-type complex or a subcomplex/cover of one.

proof_techniques.name: Use planar diagrams and dipole normal forms
proof_techniques.description: Diagram groups in the paper are controlled by planar diagrams over directed 2-complexes. Concatenation glues diagrams, and the only cancellation allowed is removal of dipoles, pairs of adjacent inverse cells. The Kilibarda normal-form theorem gives a unique reduced representative in every equivalence class. This makes it possible to prove injectivity, identify fundamental groups, and show that certain constructions do not introduce ambiguity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a matching can be represented as a planar diagram recording pairings or reductions, uniqueness after canceling obvious inverse local features could provide a canonical model for cells of F_w and prevent distinct resolution sequences from producing unwanted identifications.

proof_techniques.name: Recognize the desired complex as a universal cover of a Squier complex
proof_techniques.description: The contractibility theorem is not that every Squier complex is contractible, but that its universal cover is. The universal cover is described concretely as Sq(eKp,p̃), where eKp is the universal 2-cover of K. The lifting lemma says that every 2-path or diagram over K with top p lifts uniquely to eKp, and the rooted 2-tree property of eKp kills all spherical diagrams at the root.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F_w parametrizes reduction diagrams with a fixed boundary word and no monodromy, it may correspond more naturally to a lifted Squier component than to the base Squier complex. Establishing a unique lifting/canonical boundary condition for matchings would allow contractibility to follow from Theorem 8.5.

proof_techniques.name: Rooted 2-tree deformation retraction
proof_techniques.description: A rooted 2-tree is built by repeatedly attaching 2-cells along old top paths and new bottom arcs. Its positive-path space is filtered by the finite stages of this construction. The key deformation retract sends a positive path using newly attached cells to the path obtained by moving all new-cell parameters continuously to 0, thereby replacing each new-cell slice by its old top path. This retracts Ωn+1 onto Ωn and ultimately onto the root path.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A direct proof of contractibility of F_w might mimic this filtration: find a hierarchy of matchings or arcs in which the newest local features can be continuously/cubically retracted to older ones, fixing the previous stage.

proof_techniques.name: Collapsing scheme via the leftmost reducible or special term
proof_techniques.description: For complete directed 2-complexes, the paper constructs a discrete Morse matching on Sq(K). Each cube is scanned from left to right; if no term is special it is essential, if the first special term is an irreducible-context failure the cube is redundant, and if the first special term is a forest edge the cube is collapsible. A well-founded order based on positive reduction and moving cells to the right guarantees that each redundant cube is a free face of a unique collapsible cube after lower collapses. This yields a homotopy equivalent CW complex with only essential cells.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If F_w admits a canonical choice of a first bad crossing, first reducible submatching, or first noncanonical pairing, the same redundant/collapsible pairing could collapse F_w. Contractibility would follow if the only essential cell is a single 0-cell or if the essential subcomplex is visibly contractible.

proof_techniques.name: Tietze and subdivision invariance for directed 2-complexes
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that adding an edge identified with an existing path, replacing cells by cells whose top and bottom are homotopic through a subcomplex, and subdividing 2-cells preserve the relevant diagram groups. The proofs work by replacing cells with fixed diagrams and by observing that reduced diagrams cannot contain newly introduced edges except inside dipoles.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When encoding free reductions or matching moves, the most convenient directed 2-complex may not initially match the combinatorics of F_w. These invariance moves justify changing the presentation or subdividing local moves to align cubes with individual crossings/resolutions.

proof_techniques.name: Positive-path realization of cubes
proof_techniques.description: Instead of viewing Sq(K) only combinatorially, the paper realizes it as Ω+(K), the space of paths that may pass through 2-cells at heights t ∈ [0,1]. A thin n-cube is literally parametrized by the heights of n cell-crossings in a positive path, and setting a parameter to 0 or 1 is the same as taking the corresponding top or bottom face.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cells of F_w are defined by local resolutions of crossings. A positive-path model may help turn these binary choices into actual cubical coordinates and verify that the given attaching maps agree with standard Squier face maps.

other_useful_info: Remark 3.2 states that the realization of Sq(K) does not depend on the chosen orientation of the 2-cells; changing orientation only changes cube reference points. This is useful when relations are symmetric. Remark 8.7 records Farley's stronger result that, for finite K, the connected components of the universal cover of Sq(K) are CAT(0) cubical complexes, giving another source of contractibility. The paper emphasizes that empty paths give isolated points in Sq(K), so nonempty base paths are the substantive case. Theorem 4.3 shows that diagram groups over arbitrary directed 2-complexes are the same class as diagram groups over semigroup presentations after suitable subdivisions, so one can freely move between geometric directed-complex language and rewriting-system language. The main cited precursor for CAT(0) and contractibility of Squier universal covers is Farley [14], while the diagram normal-form and presentation machinery comes from Guba-Sapir [16].
why_relevant: Develops Squier complexes and proves contractibility results for their universal covers. F_w resembles a cubical rewriting/resolution complex for free reductions, so diagram-group techniques are a plausible route.
